infobox.value: [City of Brussels](/wiki/City_of_Brussels "City of Brussels") (partly [outside the territory](/wiki/List_of_capitals_outside_the_territories_they_serve "List of capitals outside the territories they serve"))
[Freya Van den Bossche](/wiki/Freya_Van_den_Bossche "Freya Van den Bossche") (Vooruit)

([/ˈflɑːndərz/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*FLAHN-dərz*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") or [/ˈflændərz/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*FLAN-dərz*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Vlaanderen* [[ˈvlaːndərə(n)]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-Vlaanderen.ogg "File:Nl-Vlaanderen.ogg"))[[a]](#cite_note-fn1-4) is the [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language")-speaking northern part of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), which is one of the [communities, regions and language areas of Belgium](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium"). However, there are several overlapping definitions, including ones related to culture, language, politics, and history, and sometimes involving neighbouring countries. The [demonym](/wiki/Demonym "Demonym") associated with Flanders is [Fleming](/wiki/Flemings "Flemings"), while the corresponding adjective is [Flemish](/wiki/Flemish_people "Flemish people"), which can also refer to the collective of Dutch dialects spoken in that area, or more generally the Belgian variant of Standard Dutch.

**Flanders** ([/ˈflɑːndərz/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*FLAHN-dərz*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key") or [/ˈflændərz/](/wiki/Help:IPA/English "Help:IPA/English") [*FLAN-dərz*](/wiki/Help:Pronunciation_respelling_key "Help:Pronunciation respelling key"); [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *Vlaanderen* [[ˈvlaːndərə(n)]](/wiki/Help:IPA/Dutch "Help:IPA/Dutch") [ⓘ](/wiki/File:Nl-Vlaanderen.ogg "File:Nl-Vlaanderen.ogg"))[[a]](#cite_note-fn1-4) is the [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language")-speaking northern part of [Belgium](/wiki/Belgium "Belgium"), which is one of the [communities, regions and language areas of Belgium](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium"). However, there are several overlapping definitions, including ones related to culture, language, politics, and history, and sometimes involving neighbouring countries. The [demonym](/wiki/Demonym "Demonym") associated with Flanders is [Fleming](/wiki/Flemings "Flemings"), while the corresponding adjective is [Flemish](/wiki/Flemish_people "Flemish people"), which can also refer to the collective of Dutch dialects spoken in that area, or more generally the Belgian variant of Standard Dutch.

Most Flemings live within the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region"), which is a federal state within Belgium with its own elected government. However, like Belgium itself, the official capital of Flanders is the [City of Brussels](/wiki/City_of_Brussels "City of Brussels"),[[4]](#cite_note-5) which lies within the [Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"), not the Flemish Region, and the majority of residents there are French speaking. The powers of the Flemish Government in Brussels are limited mainly to Flemish culture and education.[[5]](#cite_note-6)

Geographically, Flanders is mainly flat, and incorporates the whole coast of Belgium on the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea"). It borders the [French department](/wiki/Departments_of_France "Departments of France") of [Nord](/wiki/Nord_(French_department) "Nord (French department)") to the south-west near the coast, the Dutch provinces of [Zeeland](/wiki/Zeeland "Zeeland"), [North Brabant](/wiki/North_Brabant "North Brabant") and [Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Netherlands) "Limburg (Netherlands)") to the north and east, and the [Walloon](/wiki/Wallonia "Wallonia") provinces of [Hainaut](/wiki/Hainaut_Province "Hainaut Province"), [Walloon Brabant](/wiki/Walloon_Brabant "Walloon Brabant") and [Liège](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge_Province "Liège Province") to the south. Despite accounting for only 45% of Belgium's territory, more than half the population lives there – 6,821,770 (or 58%) out of 11,763,650 Belgian inhabitants, as of January 2024. Much of Flanders is agriculturally fertile and densely populated at 501/km2 (1,300/sq mi). The Brussels Region is an officially bilingual [enclave](/wiki/Enclave "Enclave") within the Flemish Region.[[b]](#cite_note-fn2-9) Flanders also has exclaves of its own: [Voeren](/wiki/Voeren "Voeren") in the east is between Wallonia and the Netherlands and [Baarle-Hertog](/wiki/Baarle-Hertog "Baarle-Hertog") in the north consists of 22 exclaves surrounded by the Netherlands. Not including Brussels, there are five present-day [Flemish provinces](/wiki/Provinces_of_Belgium "Provinces of Belgium"): [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp_Province "Antwerp Province"), [East Flanders](/wiki/East_Flanders "East Flanders"), [Flemish Brabant](/wiki/Flemish_Brabant "Flemish Brabant"), [Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Belgium) "Limburg (Belgium)") and [West Flanders](/wiki/West_Flanders "West Flanders"). The official language is Dutch.

The area of today's Flanders has figured prominently in European history since the [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"). The original [County of Flanders](/wiki/County_of_Flanders "County of Flanders") stretched around AD 900 from the [Strait of Dover](/wiki/Strait_of_Dover "Strait of Dover") to the [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt") [estuary](/wiki/Estuary "Estuary") and expanded from there. This county also still corresponds roughly with the modern-day Belgian provinces of West Flanders and East Flanders, along with neighbouring parts of France and the Netherlands.[[c]](#cite_note-fn3-10) In this period, cities such as [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent") and [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges") of the historic County of Flanders, and later [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") of the [Duchy of Brabant](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant") made it one of the richest and most urbanised parts of Europe, trading, and weaving the [wool](/wiki/Wool "Wool") of neighbouring lands into cloth for both domestic use and export. As a consequence, a very sophisticated culture developed, with impressive achievements in the arts and architecture, rivaling those of [northern Italy](/wiki/Northern_Italy "Northern Italy").

Belgium was one of the centres of the 19th-century [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), but this occurred mainly in French-speaking Wallonia. In the second half of the 20th century, and due to massive national investments in port infrastructure, Flanders' economy modernised rapidly, and today Flanders and Brussels are much wealthier than Wallonia, being among the wealthiest regions in Europe and the world.[[8]](#cite_note-11) In accordance with late 20th century [Belgian state reforms](/wiki/State_reform_in_Belgium "State reform in Belgium"), Flanders was made into two political entities: the Flemish Region (Dutch: *Vlaams Gewest*) and the [Flemish Community](/wiki/Flemish_Community "Flemish Community") (Dutch: *Vlaamse Gemeenschap*). These entities were merged, although geographically the Flemish Community, which has a broader cultural mandate, covers Brussels, whereas the Flemish Region does not.

## Terminology

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=1 "Edit section: Terminology")]

### Modern Belgium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=2 "Edit section: Modern Belgium")]

The term "Flanders" has several main modern meanings:

* The "Flemish community", i.e. the social, cultural and linguistic, scientific and educational, economical and political community of the [Flemings](/wiki/Flemings "Flemings"). For most purposes this is considered to include the 6.5 million Belgians (approximately 60%) who consider Dutch to be their mother tongue, including many people living in the [Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Brussels-Capital_Region "Brussels-Capital Region").
* In the context of the political subdivisions of Belgium there are the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region") (competent in mainly economic matters) and the [Flemish Community](/wiki/Flemish_Community "Flemish Community") (competent in mainly cultural matters). The first does not comprise Brussels (which forms a [Region by itself](/wiki/Brussels-Capital_Region "Brussels-Capital Region")), whereas the latter does comprise the Dutch-speaking inhabitants of Brussels.
* The political institutions that govern both subdivisions: the operative body or "[Flemish Government](/wiki/Flemish_Government "Flemish Government")", and the legislative organ or "[Flemish Parliament](/wiki/Flemish_Parliament "Flemish Parliament")".
* Within Belgian discussions, the two westernmost provinces of the Flemish Region, [West Flanders](/wiki/West_Flanders "West Flanders") and [East Flanders](/wiki/East_Flanders "East Flanders"), forming the central portion of the historic [County of Flanders](/wiki/County_of_Flanders "County of Flanders") are also still collectively referred to as Flanders.

### Historical

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=3 "Edit section: Historical")]

Main article: [Terminology of the Low Countries § Flanders](/wiki/Terminology_of_the_Low_Countries#Flanders "Terminology of the Low Countries")

The name originally applied to the *[ancien régime](/wiki/Ancien_r%C3%A9gime "Ancien régime")* territory called the [County of Flanders](/wiki/County_of_Flanders "County of Flanders"), that existed from the 8th century (Latin *Flandria*) until its absorption by the [French First Republic](/wiki/French_First_Republic "French First Republic"). Until the 1600s, this county also extended over parts of what are now France and the Netherlands.

* In France, one of the historically Flemish regions is now in the [Nord department](/wiki/Nord_(French_department) "Nord (French department)"). This is referred to as [French Flanders](/wiki/French_Flanders "French Flanders"), and can be divided into two smaller regions: [Walloon Flanders](/wiki/Walloon_Flanders "Walloon Flanders") and [Maritime Flanders (Westhoek)](/wiki/Westhoek_(region) "Westhoek (region)"). The first region was predominantly French-speaking already in the 1600s, the latter became so in the 20th century. The [city of Lille](/wiki/Lille "Lille") identifies itself as "Flemish", and this is reflected, for instance, in the name of its local railway station [TGV](/wiki/TGV "TGV") *[Lille Flandres](/wiki/Gare_de_Lille_Flandres "Gare de Lille Flandres")*.
* The historically Flemish region which became part of the [Dutch Republic](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic"), now part of the Dutch province of [Zeeland](/wiki/Zeeland "Zeeland"), called [Zeelandic Flanders](/wiki/Zeelandic_Flanders "Zeelandic Flanders").

However, the term came to be used for a bigger territory, and this is critical to the evolution of modern terminology. Once the Counts of Flanders (who were also Dukes of Burgundy) expanded their regional power to create the bigger entity, now referred to by historians as the [Burgundian Netherlands](/wiki/Burgundian_Netherlands "Burgundian Netherlands"), "Flanders", along with Latin "Belgium", were the first two common names to describe this regional block.[[9]](#cite_note-12) With the breakaway of the northern Netherlands in the [early modern period](/wiki/Early_modern_period "Early modern period"), the term Flanders continued to be associated with the whole southern part of the [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries")—the [Southern](/wiki/Southern_Netherlands "Southern Netherlands"), [Spanish](/wiki/Spanish_Netherlands "Spanish Netherlands") or [Austrian Netherlands](/wiki/Austrian_Netherlands "Austrian Netherlands"), which were the successors of the Burgundian state, and also predecessors of modern Belgium. The restriction of the term Flanders to the Germanic speaking part of the population occurred later.

### Dutch-speaking part of Belgium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=4 "Edit section: Dutch-speaking part of Belgium")]

The term "Flemish" came to be a term for the language Dutch, and during the 19th and 20th centuries, it became increasingly common to refer exclusively to the Dutch-speaking part of Belgium as "Flanders". Belgium divided itself into official French- and Dutch-speaking parts starting in the early 1960s. Today Flanders extends over the northern part of Belgium, including not only the Dutch-speaking Belgian parts of the medieval [Duchy of Brabant](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant"), which was united with Flanders since the Middle Ages, but also [Belgian Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Belgium) "Limburg (Belgium)"), which corresponds closely to the medieval [County of Loon](/wiki/County_of_Loon "County of Loon"), and was never under Burgundian control.

The ambiguity between this wider cultural area and that of the county or province still remains in discussions about the region. In most present-day contexts however, the term Flanders is taken to refer to either the political, social, cultural, and linguistic community (and the corresponding official institution, the [Flemish Community](/wiki/Flemish_Community "Flemish Community")), or the geographical area, one of the three institutional regions in Belgium, namely the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region").

In the [history of art](/wiki/History_of_art "History of art") and other fields, the adjectives Flemish and [Netherlandish](/wiki/Netherlandish "Netherlandish") are commonly used to designate all the artistic production in this area before about 1580, after which it refers specifically to the southern Netherlands. For example, the term "Flemish Primitives", now outdated in English but used in French, Dutch and other languages, is a synonym for "[Early Netherlandish painting](/wiki/Early_Netherlandish_painting "Early Netherlandish painting")", and it is not uncommon to see [Mosan art](/wiki/Mosan_art "Mosan art") categorized as Flemish art. In music the *[Franco-Flemish School](/wiki/Franco-Flemish_School "Franco-Flemish School")* is also known as the *Dutch School*.

Within this Dutch-speaking part of Belgium, French has never ceased to be spoken by some citizens, and Jewish groups have been speaking [Yiddish](/wiki/Yiddish "Yiddish") in Antwerp for centuries. Regardless of nationality or linguistic background, according to Belgian Law education in schools located in the Flemish Region must be mainly in the Dutch language. In Brussels, teaching is also done in French.

## History

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=5 "Edit section: History")]

Main article: [History of Flanders](/wiki/History_of_Flanders "History of Flanders")

### Early history

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=6 "Edit section: Early history")]

Main article: [Belgae](/wiki/Belgae "Belgae")

When [Julius Caesar](/wiki/Julius_Caesar "Julius Caesar") conquered the area he described it as the less economically developed and more warlike part of *[Gallia Belgica](/wiki/Gallia_Belgica "Gallia Belgica")*. His informants told him that especially in the east, the tribes claimed ancestral connections and kinship with the ["Germanic" peoples](/wiki/Germanic_peoples "Germanic peoples") then east of the [Rhine](/wiki/Rhine "Rhine"). Under the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire") the whole of *Gallia Belgica* became an administrative province. The future counties of Flanders and Brabant remained part of this province connected to what is now France, but in the east modern Limburg became part of the Rhine frontier province of *[Germania Inferior](/wiki/Germania_Inferior "Germania Inferior")* connected to what is now the Netherlands and Germany. *Gallia Belgica* and *Germania Inferior* were the two most northerly continental provinces of the [Roman Empire](/wiki/Roman_Empire "Roman Empire").

In the future county of Flanders, the main [Belgic tribe](/wiki/Belgic_tribe "Belgic tribe") in early Roman times was the [Menapii](/wiki/Menapii "Menapii"), but also on the coast were the [Marsacii](/wiki/Marsacii "Marsacii") and [Morini](/wiki/Morini "Morini"). In the central part of modern Belgium were the [Nervii](/wiki/Nervii "Nervii"), whose territory corresponded to medieval Brabant as well as French-speaking Hainaut. In the east was the large district of the [Tungri](/wiki/Tungri "Tungri") which covered both French- and Dutch-speaking parts of eastern Belgium. The Tungri were understood to have links to Germanic tribes east of the Rhine. Another notable group were the [Toxandrians](/wiki/Toxandrians "Toxandrians") who appear to have lived in the [Kempen](/wiki/Campine "Campine") region, in the northern parts of both the Nervian and Tungrian districts, probably stretching into the modern Netherlands. The Roman administrative districts (*[civitates](/wiki/Civitas "Civitas")*) of the Menapii, Nervii and Tungri therefore corresponded roughly with the medieval counties of Flanders, Brabant and [Loon](/wiki/County_of_Loon "County of Loon"), and the modern Flemish provinces of East and West Flanders (Menapii), Brabant and Antwerp (the northern Nervii), and Belgian Limburg (part of the Tungri). Brabant appears to have been separated from the Tungri by a relatively unpopulated forest area, the [Silva Carbonaria](/wiki/Silva_Carbonaria "Silva Carbonaria"), forming a natural boundary between northeast and southwest Belgium.

Linguistically, the tribes in this area were under [Celtic](/wiki/Celtic_languages "Celtic languages") influence in the south, and [Germanic](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages") influence in the east, but there is disagreement about what languages were spoken locally (apart from [Vulgar Latin](/wiki/Vulgar_Latin "Vulgar Latin")), and there may even have been an intermediate "[Nordwestblock](/wiki/Nordwestblock "Nordwestblock")" language related to both. By the first century AD, [Germanic languages](/wiki/Germanic_languages "Germanic languages") appear to have become prevalent in the area of the Tungri.

As Roman influence waned, Frankish populations settled in the Tungiran area east of the Silva Carbonaria, and eventually pushed through it under [Chlodio](/wiki/Chlodio "Chlodio"). They had kings in each Roman district (*civitas*). In the meantime, the Franks contributed to the Roman military. The first Merovingian king [Childeric I](/wiki/Childeric_I "Childeric I") was king of the Franks within the military of Gaul. He became leader of the administration of *Belgica Secunda*, which included the *civitas* of the Menapii (the future county of Flanders). From there, his son [Clovis I](/wiki/Clovis_I "Clovis I") managed to conquer both the Roman populations of northern France and the Frankish populations beyond the forest areas.

### Historical Flanders

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=7 "Edit section: Historical Flanders")]

Main article: [County of Flanders](/wiki/County_of_Flanders "County of Flanders")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/9/98/Archive-ugent-be-79D46426-CC9D-11E3-B56B-4FBAD43445F2_DS-90_%28cropped%29.jpg/250px-Archive-ugent-be-79D46426-CC9D-11E3-B56B-4FBAD43445F2_DS-90_%28cropped%29.jpg)](/wiki/File:Archive-ugent-be-79D46426-CC9D-11E3-B56B-4FBAD43445F2_DS-90_(cropped).jpg)

A Flemish lady and gentleman in the year 1400, illustrated in the manuscript "Théâtre de tous les peuples et nations de la terre avec leurs habits et ornemens divers, tant anciens que modernes, diligemment depeints au naturel". Painted by [Lucas d'Heere](/wiki/Lucas_de_Heere "Lucas de Heere") in the 2nd half of the 16th century. Preserved in the [Ghent University Library](/wiki/Ghent_University_Library "Ghent University Library").[[10]](#cite_note-13)

The County of Flanders was a [feudal](/wiki/Feudal "Feudal") [fief](/wiki/Fief "Fief") in [West Francia](/wiki/West_Francia "West Francia"). The first certain Count in the comital family, [Baldwin I of Flanders](/wiki/Baldwin_I_of_Flanders "Baldwin I of Flanders"), is first reported in a document of 862, when he eloped with a daughter of his king [Charles the Bald](/wiki/Charles_the_Bald "Charles the Bald"). The region developed as a medieval economic power with a large degree of political autonomy. While its trading cities remained strong, it was weakened and divided when districts fell under direct French royal rule in the late 12th century. The remaining parts of Flanders came under the rule of the counts of neighbouring imperial [Hainaut](/wiki/County_of_Hainaut "County of Hainaut") under [Baldwin V of Hainaut](/wiki/Baldwin_V_of_Hainaut "Baldwin V of Hainaut") in 1191.

During the late [Middle Ages](/wiki/Middle_Ages "Middle Ages"), Flanders's trading towns (notably [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges") and [Ypres](/wiki/Ypres "Ypres")) made it one of the richest and most urbanized parts of Europe, weaving the [wool](/wiki/Wool "Wool") of neighbouring lands into cloth for both domestic use and export. As a consequence, a sophisticated culture developed, with impressive art and architecture, rivaling those of northern Italy. Ghent, Bruges, Ypres and the [Franc of Bruges](/wiki/Franc_of_Bruges "Franc of Bruges") formed the [Four Members](/wiki/Four_Members "Four Members"), a form of parliament that exercised considerable power in Flanders.[[11]](#cite_note-14)

Increasingly powerful from the 12th century, the territory's autonomous urban [communes](/wiki/Medieval_commune "Medieval commune") were instrumental in defeating a French attempt at annexation (1300–1302), finally defeating the French in the [Battle of the Golden Spurs](/wiki/Battle_of_the_Golden_Spurs "Battle of the Golden Spurs") (11 July 1302), near [Kortrijk](/wiki/Kortrijk "Kortrijk"). Two years later, the [uprising was defeated](/wiki/Franco-Flemish_War_(1297-1305) "Franco-Flemish War (1297-1305)") and Flanders indirectly remained part of the French Crown. Flemish prosperity waned in the following century, due to widespread European population decline following the [Black Death](/wiki/Black_Death "Black Death") of 1348, the disruption of trade during the Anglo-French [Hundred Years' War](/wiki/Hundred_Years%27_War "Hundred Years' War") (1337–1453), and increased English cloth production. Flemish weavers had gone over to [Worstead](/wiki/Worstead "Worstead") and [North Walsham](/wiki/North_Walsham "North Walsham") in Norfolk in the 12th century and established the woolen industry.

The County of Flanders started to take control of the neighbouring [County of Brabant](/wiki/County_of_Brabant "County of Brabant") during the life of [Louis II, Count of Flanders](/wiki/Louis_II,_Count_of_Flanders "Louis II, Count of Flanders") (1330–1384), who fought his sister-in-law [Joanna, Duchess of Brabant](/wiki/Joanna,_Duchess_of_Brabant "Joanna, Duchess of Brabant") for control of it.

The entire area, straddling the ancient boundary of France and the Holy Roman Empire, later passed to [Philip the Bold](/wiki/Philip_the_Bold "Philip the Bold") in 1384, the [Duke of Burgundy](/wiki/Duchy_of_Burgundy "Duchy of Burgundy"), with his capital in [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"). The titles were eventually more clearly united under his grandson [Philip the Good](/wiki/Philip_the_Good "Philip the Good") (1396 – 1467). This large Duchy passed in [1477](/wiki/Mary_of_Burgundy "Mary of Burgundy") to the [Habsburg](/wiki/Habsburg "Habsburg") dynasty, and in [1556](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain") to the kings of Spain. Western and southern districts of Flanders were confirmed under French rule under successive treaties of [1659](/wiki/Treaty_of_the_Pyrenees "Treaty of the Pyrenees") (Artois), [1668](/wiki/Treaty_of_Aix-la-Chapelle_(1668) "Treaty of Aix-la-Chapelle (1668)") and [1678](/wiki/Treaties_of_Nijmegen "Treaties of Nijmegen").

The County of Loon, approximately the modern Flemish province of Limburg, remained independent of France, forming a part of the [Prince-Bishopric of Liège](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Prince-Bishopric of Liège") until the French Revolution, but surrounded by the Burgundians, and under their influence.

### Low Countries

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=8 "Edit section: Low Countries")]

Main article: [Low Countries](/wiki/Low_Countries "Low Countries")

#### Beeldenstorm

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=9 "Edit section: Beeldenstorm")]

In 1500, [Charles V](/wiki/Charles_V,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Charles V, Holy Roman Emperor") was born in [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"). He inherited the [Seventeen Provinces](/wiki/Seventeen_Provinces "Seventeen Provinces") (1506), Spain (1516) with its colonies and in 1519 was elected [Holy Roman Emperor](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Emperor "Holy Roman Emperor").[[12]](#cite_note-15) Charles V issued the [Pragmatic Sanction of 1549](/wiki/Pragmatic_Sanction_of_1549 "Pragmatic Sanction of 1549"), which established the Low Countries as the Seventeen Provinces (or [Spanish Netherlands](/wiki/Spanish_Netherlands "Spanish Netherlands") in its broad sense) as an entity separate from the [Holy Roman Empire](/wiki/Holy_Roman_Empire "Holy Roman Empire") and from France. In 1556 Charles V abdicated due to ill health (he suffered from crippling [gout](/wiki/Gout "Gout")).[[13]](#cite_note-16) Spain and the Seventeen Provinces went to his son, [Philip II of Spain](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain").

Over the first half of the 16th century [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") grew to become the second-largest European city north of the [Alps](/wiki/Alps "Alps") by 1560. Antwerp was the richest city in Europe at this time.[[14]](#cite_note-17) According to Luc-Normand Tellier "It is estimated that the port of Antwerp was earning the Spanish crown seven times more revenues than the [Americas](/wiki/Spanish_colonization_of_the_Americas "Spanish colonization of the Americas")."[[15]](#cite_note-18)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/8e/Incendio_Ayuntamiento_Amberes.jpg/250px-Incendio_Ayuntamiento_Amberes.jpg)](/wiki/File:Incendio_Ayuntamiento_Amberes.jpg)

The [Sack of Antwerp](/wiki/Sack_of_Antwerp "Sack of Antwerp") in 1576, in which about 7,000 people died

Meanwhile, Protestantism had reached the Low Countries. Among the wealthy traders of Antwerp, the [Lutheran](/wiki/Lutheranism "Lutheranism") beliefs of the German [Hanseatic](/wiki/Hanseatic_(class) "Hanseatic (class)") traders found appeal, perhaps partly for economic reasons. The spread of Protestantism in this city was aided by the presence of an [Augustinian](/wiki/Augustinians "Augustinians") cloister (founded 1514) in the St. Andries quarter. Luther, an Augustinian himself, had taught some of the monks, and his works were in print by 1518. The first Lutheran martyrs came from Antwerp. The [Reformation](/wiki/Protestant_Reformation "Protestant Reformation") resulted in consecutive but overlapping waves of reform: a Lutheran, followed by a militant [Anabaptist](/wiki/Anabaptist "Anabaptist"), then a [Mennonite](/wiki/Mennonite "Mennonite"), and finally a [Calvinistic](/wiki/Calvinism "Calvinism") movement. These movements existed independently of each other.

[Philip II](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain"), a devout Catholic and self-proclaimed protector of the [Counter-Reformation](/wiki/Counter-Reformation "Counter-Reformation"), [suppressed](/wiki/Suppression_of_dissent "Suppression of dissent") Calvinism in Flanders, [Brabant](/wiki/Duchy_of_Brabant "Duchy of Brabant") and Holland (what is now approximately [Belgian Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Belgium) "Limburg (Belgium)") was part of the [Prince-Bishopric of Liège](/wiki/Prince-Bishopric_of_Li%C3%A8ge "Prince-Bishopric of Liège") and was Catholic *de facto*). In 1566, the wave of [iconoclasm](/wiki/Iconoclasm "Iconoclasm") known as the *[Beeldenstorm](/wiki/Beeldenstorm "Beeldenstorm")* was a prelude to religious war between Catholics and Protestants, especially the Anabaptists. The *Beeldenstorm* started in what is now [French Flanders](/wiki/French_Flanders "French Flanders"), with open-air sermons ([Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"): *hagepreken*) that spread through the Low Countries, first to Antwerp and Ghent, and from there further east and north.

#### The Eighty Years' War and its consequences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=10 "Edit section: The Eighty Years' War and its consequences")]

Subsequently, Philip II of Spain sent [the Duke](/wiki/Fernando_%C3%81lvarez_de_Toledo,_3rd_Duke_of_Alba "Fernando Álvarez de Toledo, 3rd Duke of Alba") of [Alba](/wiki/Duchy_of_Alba "Duchy of Alba") to the Provinces to repress the revolt. Alba recaptured the southern part of the Provinces, who signed the [Union of Atrecht](/wiki/Union_of_Arras "Union of Arras"), which meant that they would accept the Spanish government on condition of more freedom. But the northern part of the provinces signed the [Union of Utrecht](/wiki/Union_of_Utrecht "Union of Utrecht") and settled in 1581 the [Republic of the Seven United Netherlands](/wiki/Republic_of_the_Seven_United_Netherlands "Republic of the Seven United Netherlands"). Spanish troops quickly started fighting the rebels, and the Spanish armies conquered the important trading cities of Bruges and Ghent. Antwerp, which was then the most important port in the world, also had to be conquered. But before the revolt was defeated, a war between Spain and England broke out, forcing Spanish troops to halt their advance. On 17 August 1585, Antwerp fell. This ended the Eighty Years' War for the (from now on) [Southern Netherlands](/wiki/Southern_Netherlands "Southern Netherlands"). The [United Provinces](/wiki/Dutch_Republic "Dutch Republic") (the Northern Netherlands) fought on until 1648 – the [Peace of Westphalia](/wiki/Peace_of_Westphalia "Peace of Westphalia").

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/4/41/Het_Kranenhoofd_aan_de_Schelde_te_Antwerpen_Rijksmuseum_SK-A-1699.jpeg/250px-Het_Kranenhoofd_aan_de_Schelde_te_Antwerpen_Rijksmuseum_SK-A-1699.jpeg)](/wiki/File:Het_Kranenhoofd_aan_de_Schelde_te_Antwerpen_Rijksmuseum_SK-A-1699.jpeg)

Winter scene at the Scheldt river in Antwerp by [Sebastian Vrancx](/wiki/Sebastian_Vrancx "Sebastian Vrancx"), 1622

During the war with England, the rebels from the north, strengthened by refugees from the south, started a campaign to reclaim areas lost to [Philip II](/wiki/Philip_II_of_Spain "Philip II of Spain")'s Spanish troops. They conquered a considerable part of Brabant (the later [North Brabant](/wiki/North_Brabant "North Brabant") of the Netherlands), and the south bank of the Scheldt estuary ([Zeelandic Flanders](/wiki/Zeelandic_Flanders "Zeelandic Flanders")), before being stopped by Spanish troops. The front at the end of this war stabilized and became the border between present-day Belgium and the Netherlands. The Dutch (as they later became known) had managed to reclaim enough of Spanish-controlled Flanders to close off the river [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt"), effectively cutting Antwerp off from its trade routes.

The [fall of Antwerp](/wiki/Fall_of_Antwerp "Fall of Antwerp") to the Spanish and the closing of the [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt") caused considerable emigration.[[d]](#cite_note-19) Many Calvinist merchants of Antwerp and other Flemish cities left Flanders and migrated north. Many of them settled in [Amsterdam](/wiki/Amsterdam "Amsterdam"), which was a smaller port, important only in the [Baltic trade](/w/index.php?title=Baltic_trade&action=edit&redlink=1 "Baltic trade (page does not exist)"). The Flemish exiles helped to rapidly transform Amsterdam into one of the world's most important ports. This is why the exodus is sometimes described as "*creating a new Antwerp*".

Flanders and Brabant, went into a period of relative decline from the time of the [Thirty Years' War](/wiki/Thirty_Years%27_War "Thirty Years' War").[[16]](#cite_note-FiifAntwHist-20) In the Northern Netherlands, the mass emigration from Flanders and Brabant became an important driving force behind the [Dutch Golden Age](/wiki/Dutch_Golden_Age "Dutch Golden Age").

#### Southern Netherlands (1581–1795)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=11 "Edit section: Southern Netherlands (1581–1795)")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3c/Flandria_%28Vlaanderen_Flanders%29_1584_Map_by_Abraham_Ortelius.jpg/250px-Flandria_%28Vlaanderen_Flanders%29_1584_Map_by_Abraham_Ortelius.jpg)](/wiki/File:Flandria_(Vlaanderen_Flanders)_1584_Map_by_Abraham_Ortelius.jpg)

1584 map of the county of Flanders

Although arts remained relatively impressive for another century with [Peter Paul Rubens](/wiki/Peter_Paul_Rubens "Peter Paul Rubens") (1577–1640) and [Anthony van Dyck](/wiki/Anthony_van_Dyck "Anthony van Dyck"), Flanders lost its former economic and intellectual power under Spanish, Austrian, and French rule. Heavy taxation and rigid imperial political control compounded the effects of industrial stagnation and Spanish-Dutch and Franco-Austrian conflict. The Southern Netherlands suffered severely under the [Franco-Dutch War](/wiki/Franco-Dutch_War "Franco-Dutch War"), [Nine Years' War](/wiki/Nine_Years%27_War "Nine Years' War") and [War of the Spanish Succession](/wiki/War_of_the_Spanish_Succession "War of the Spanish Succession"). But under the reign of Empress Maria-Theresia, these lands again flourished economically. Influenced by [the Enlightenment](/wiki/Age_of_Enlightenment "Age of Enlightenment"), the Austrian Emperor [Joseph II](/wiki/Joseph_II,_Holy_Roman_Emperor "Joseph II, Holy Roman Emperor") was the first sovereign who had been in the Southern Netherlands since King Philip II of Spain left them in 1559.

#### French Revolution and Napoleonic France (1795–1815)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=12 "Edit section: French Revolution and Napoleonic France (1795–1815)")]

In 1794, the [French Republican Army](/wiki/History_of_France#Revolution "History of France") started using Antwerp as the northernmost naval port of France.[[16]](#cite_note-FiifAntwHist-20) The following year, France officially annexed Flanders as the *[départements](/wiki/The_130_d%C3%A9partements "The 130 départements")* of [Lys](/wiki/Lys_(d%C3%A9partement) "Lys (département)"), [Escaut](/wiki/Escaut_(d%C3%A9partement) "Escaut (département)"), [Deux-Nèthes](/wiki/Deux-N%C3%A8thes "Deux-Nèthes"), [Meuse-Inférieure](/wiki/Meuse-Inf%C3%A9rieure "Meuse-Inférieure") and [Dyle](/wiki/Dyle_(d%C3%A9partement) "Dyle (département)"). Obligatory (French) army service for all men aged 16–25 years was a main reason for the uprising against the French in 1798, known as the *Boerenkrijg* (*Peasants' War*), with the heaviest fighting in the [Campine](/wiki/Campine "Campine") area.

#### United Kingdom of the Netherlands (1815–1830)

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=13 "Edit section: United Kingdom of the Netherlands (1815–1830)")]

After the defeat of [Napoleon Bonaparte](/wiki/Napoleon_I_of_France "Napoleon I of France") at the 1815 [Battle of Waterloo](/wiki/Battle_of_Waterloo "Battle of Waterloo") in [Brabant](/wiki/Province_of_Brabant "Province of Brabant"), the [Congress of Vienna](/wiki/Congress_of_Vienna "Congress of Vienna") (1815) gave sovereignty over the [Austrian Netherlands](/wiki/Austrian_Netherlands "Austrian Netherlands") – Belgium minus the [East Cantons](/wiki/East_Cantons "East Cantons") and Luxembourg – to the [United Netherlands](/wiki/Sovereign_Principality_of_the_United_Netherlands "Sovereign Principality of the United Netherlands") (Dutch: *Verenigde Nederlanden*) under Prince William I of Orange Nassau, making him [William I of the United Kingdom of the Netherlands](/wiki/William_I_of_the_Netherlands "William I of the Netherlands"). William I started rapid industrialisation of the southern parts of the Kingdom. But the political system failed to forge a true union between the north and south. Most of the southern [bourgeoisie](/wiki/Bourgeoisie "Bourgeoisie") was Roman Catholic and French-speaking, while the north was mainly Protestant and Dutch-speaking.

In 1815, the Dutch Senate was reinstated (Dutch: *Eerste Kamer der Staaten Generaal*). The nobility, mainly coming from the south, became increasingly estranged from their northern colleagues. Resentment grew between the Roman Catholics from the south and the Protestants from the north, and also between the powerful liberal bourgeoisie from the south and their more moderate colleagues from the north. On 25 August 1830 (after the showing of the opera '[La Muette de Portici](/wiki/La_Muette_de_Portici "La Muette de Portici")' of [Daniel Auber](/wiki/Daniel_Auber "Daniel Auber") in Brussels) the [Belgian Revolution](/wiki/Belgian_Revolution "Belgian Revolution") sparked. On 4 October 1830, the [Provisional Government](/wiki/Provisional_Government_of_Belgium "Provisional Government of Belgium") (Dutch: *Voorlopig Bewind*) proclaimed its independence, which was later confirmed by the [National Congress](/wiki/National_Congress_of_Belgium "National Congress of Belgium") that issued a new Liberal Constitution and declared the new state a [Constitutional Monarchy](/wiki/Constitutional_Monarchy "Constitutional Monarchy"), under the House of [Saxe-Coburg](/wiki/Saxe-Coburg "Saxe-Coburg"). Flanders now became part of the Kingdom of Belgium, which was recognized by the major European Powers on 20 January 1831. The cessation was recognized by the [United Kingdom of the Netherlands](/wiki/United_Kingdom_of_the_Netherlands "United Kingdom of the Netherlands") on 19 April 1839.

### Kingdom of Belgium

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=14 "Edit section: Kingdom of Belgium")]

Further information: [History of Belgium](/wiki/History_of_Belgium "History of Belgium")

In 1830, the [Belgian Revolution](/wiki/Belgian_Revolution "Belgian Revolution") led to the splitting up of the two countries. Belgium was confirmed as an independent state by the [Treaty of London](/wiki/Treaty_of_London,_1839 "Treaty of London, 1839") of 1839, but deprived of the eastern half of Limburg (now Dutch Limburg), and the Eastern half of Luxembourg (now the Grand-Duchy of Luxembourg). Sovereignty over [Zeelandic Flanders](/wiki/Zeelandic_Flanders "Zeelandic Flanders"), south of the [Westerscheldt](/wiki/Westerscheldt "Westerscheldt") river delta, was left with the Kingdom of the Netherlands, which was allowed to levy a toll on all traffic to Antwerp harbour until 1863.[[16]](#cite_note-FiifAntwHist-20)[[17]](#cite_note-21)

#### Rise of the Flemish Movement

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=15 "Edit section: Rise of the Flemish Movement")]

Main article: [Flemish Movement](/wiki/Flemish_Movement "Flemish Movement")

In 1873, Dutch became an official language in public secondary schools. In 1898, Dutch and French were declared equal languages in laws and Royal orders. In 1930, the first Flemish university was opened.[[18]](#cite_note-22)

The first official translation of the Belgian constitution in Dutch was not published until 1967.

#### World War I and its consequences

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=16 "Edit section: World War I and its consequences")]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/7d/Gesneuvelden_Koksijde_-_Belgi%C3%AB.jpg/250px-Gesneuvelden_Koksijde_-_Belgi%C3%AB.jpg)](/wiki/File:Gesneuvelden_Koksijde_-_Belgi%C3%AB.jpg)

[Koksijde](/wiki/Koksijde "Koksijde"), a memorial to soldiers killed in [World War I](/wiki/World_War_I "World War I")

Flanders (and Belgium as a whole) saw some of the greatest loss of life on the [Western Front](/wiki/Western_Front_(World_War_I) "Western Front (World War I)") of the [First World War](/wiki/First_World_War "First World War"), in particular from the three battles of [Ypres](/wiki/Ypres "Ypres").

The war strengthened Flemish identity and consciousness. The occupying German authorities took several Flemish-friendly measures. The resulting suffering of the war is remembered by Flemish organizations during the yearly [Yser pilgrimage](/wiki/Ijzerbedevaart "Ijzerbedevaart") in [Diksmuide](/wiki/Diksmuide "Diksmuide") at the monument of the [Yser Tower](/wiki/IJzertoren "IJzertoren").

#### Right-wing nationalism in the interbellum and World War II

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=17 "Edit section: Right-wing nationalism in the interbellum and World War II")]

Main articles: [Flemish National Union](/wiki/Flemish_National_Union "Flemish National Union"), [Verdinaso](/wiki/Verdinaso "Verdinaso"), [Dietsland](/wiki/Dietsland "Dietsland"), and [Cyriel Verschaeve](/wiki/Cyriel_Verschaeve "Cyriel Verschaeve")

During the interbellum and [World War II](/wiki/World_War_II "World War II"), several right-wing [fascist](/wiki/Fascist "Fascist") and/or national-socialistic parties emerged in Belgium. Since these parties were promised more rights for the Flemings by the German government during World War II, many of them collaborated with the Nazi regime. After the war, collaborators (or people who were *Zwart*, "Black" during the war) were prosecuted and punished, among them many Flemish nationalists whose main political goal had been the emancipation of Flanders. As a result, until today [Flemish nationalism](/wiki/Flemish_nationalism "Flemish nationalism") is often associated with [right-wing politics](/wiki/Right-wing_politics "Right-wing politics"). Flemish nationalism is however a direct consequence of the events of the years prior to the first World War, in which many were oppressed by the French speaking majority. This ultimately gave way to a rising feeling of cultural autonomy and even a sense of a nationalism.

#### Flemish autonomy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=18 "Edit section: Flemish autonomy")]

Main articles: [State reform in Belgium](/wiki/State_reform_in_Belgium "State reform in Belgium"), [2007–2011 Belgian political crisis](/wiki/2007%E2%80%932011_Belgian_political_crisis "2007–2011 Belgian political crisis"), and [Partition of Belgium](/wiki/Partition_of_Belgium "Partition of Belgium")

After World War II, the differences between Dutch-speaking and French-speaking Belgians became clear in a number of conflicts, such as the [Royal Question](/wiki/Royal_Question "Royal Question"), the question whether King Leopold III should return (which most Flemings supported but Walloons did not) and the use of Dutch in the [Catholic University of Leuven](/wiki/Catholic_University_of_Leuven_(1834%E2%80%931968) "Catholic University of Leuven (1834–1968)"). As a result, several [state reforms](/wiki/State_reform_in_Belgium "State reform in Belgium") took place in the second half of the 20th century, which transformed the unitary Belgium into a federal state with [communities, regions and language areas](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium"). This resulted also in the establishment of a [Flemish Parliament](/wiki/Flemish_Parliament "Flemish Parliament") and [Government](/wiki/Flemish_Government "Flemish Government"). During the 1970s, all major political parties split into a Dutch and French-speaking party.

Several Flemish parties still advocate for more Flemish autonomy, some even for Flemish independence (see [Partition of Belgium](/wiki/Partition_of_Belgium "Partition of Belgium")), whereas the French-speakers would like to keep the current state as it is. Recent governments (such as [Verhofstadt I Government](/wiki/Verhofstadt_I_Government "Verhofstadt I Government")) have transferred certain federal competences to the regional governments.

On 13 December 2006, a [spoof news broadcast](/wiki/Flemish_Secession_hoax "Flemish Secession hoax") by the Belgian Francophone public broadcasting station [RTBF](/wiki/RTBF "RTBF") announced that Flanders had decided to declare independence from Belgium.

The [2007 federal elections](/wiki/Belgian_federal_election,_2007 "Belgian federal election, 2007") showed more support for Flemish autonomy, marking the start of the [2007–2011 Belgian political crisis](/wiki/2007%E2%80%932011_Belgian_political_crisis "2007–2011 Belgian political crisis"). All the political parties that advocated a significant increase of Flemish autonomy gained votes as well as seats in the [Belgian federal parliament](/wiki/Belgian_federal_parliament "Belgian federal parliament"). This was especially the case for [Christian Democratic and Flemish](/wiki/Christen-Democratisch_en_Vlaams "Christen-Democratisch en Vlaams") and [New Flemish Alliance](/wiki/New_Flemish_Alliance "New Flemish Alliance") (N-VA) (who had participated on a shared [electoral list](/wiki/Electoral_list "Electoral list")). The trend continued during the [2009 regional elections](/wiki/Belgian_regional_elections,_2009 "Belgian regional elections, 2009"), where CD&V and N-VA were the clear winners in Flanders, and N-VA became even the largest party in Flanders and Belgium during the [2010 federal elections](/wiki/Belgian_federal_election,_2010 "Belgian federal election, 2010"), followed by the [longest-ever government formation](/wiki/2010%E2%80%932011_Belgian_government_formation "2010–2011 Belgian government formation") after which the [Di Rupo I Government](/wiki/Di_Rupo_I_Government "Di Rupo I Government") was formed excluding N-VA. Eight parties agreed on a [sixth state reform](/wiki/Sixth_Belgian_state_reform "Sixth Belgian state reform") which aim to solve the disputes between Flemings and French-speakers. However, the [2012 provincial and municipal elections](/wiki/Belgian_provincial_and_municipal_elections,_2012 "Belgian provincial and municipal elections, 2012") continued the trend of N-VA becoming the biggest party in Flanders.

However, sociological studies show no parallel between the rise of nationalist parties and popular support for their agenda. Instead, a recent study revealed a majority in favour of returning regional competences to the federal level.[[19]](#cite_note-23)

## Government and politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=19 "Edit section: Government and politics")]

Main article: [Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium](/wiki/Communities,_regions_and_language_areas_of_Belgium "Communities, regions and language areas of Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/d/df/Kris_Peeters_675.jpg/250px-Kris_Peeters_675.jpg)](/wiki/File:Kris_Peeters_675.jpg)

[Kris Peeters](/wiki/Kris_Peeters "Kris Peeters"), former [Minister-President of Flanders](/wiki/Minister-President_of_Flanders "Minister-President of Flanders"), promoting [Flanders in Action](/wiki/Flanders_in_Action "Flanders in Action")

Both the [Flemish Community](/wiki/Flemish_Community "Flemish Community") and the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region") are constitutional institutions of the Kingdom of Belgium, exercising certain powers within their jurisdiction, granted following a series of [state reforms](/wiki/State_reform_in_Belgium "State reform in Belgium"). In practice, the Flemish Community and Region together form a single body, with its own [parliament](/wiki/Flemish_Parliament "Flemish Parliament") and [government](/wiki/Flemish_Government "Flemish Government"), as the Community legally absorbed the competences of the Region. The parliament is a directly elected legislative body composed of 124 representatives. The government consists of up to 11 members and is presided by a [Minister-President](/wiki/Minister-President_of_Flanders "Minister-President of Flanders"), currently [Geert Bourgeois](/wiki/Geert_Bourgeois "Geert Bourgeois") ([New Flemish Alliance](/wiki/New_Flemish_Alliance "New Flemish Alliance")) leading a coalition of his party (N-VA) with [Christen-Democratisch en Vlaams](/wiki/Christen-Democratisch_en_Vlaams "Christen-Democratisch en Vlaams") (CD&V) and [Open Vlaamse Liberalen en Democraten](/wiki/Open_Vlaamse_Liberalen_en_Democraten "Open Vlaamse Liberalen en Democraten") (Open VLD).

The area of the Flemish Community is represented on the maps above, including the area of the [Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Brussels-Capital_Region "Brussels-Capital Region") (hatched on the relevant map). Roughly, the Flemish Community exercises competences originally oriented towards the individuals of the Community's language: culture (including audiovisual media), [education](/wiki/Flemish_education "Flemish education"), and the use of the language. Extensions to personal matters less directly associated with language comprise sports, health policy (curative and preventive medicine), and assistance to individuals (protection of youth, social welfare, aid to families, immigrant assistance services, etc.)[[20]](#cite_note-fedgov2-24)

The area of the Flemish Region is represented on the maps above. It has a population of more than 6 million (excluding the Dutch-speaking community in the Brussels Region, grey on the map for it is not a part of the Flemish Region). Roughly, the Flemish Region is responsible for territorial issues in a broad sense, including economy, employment, agriculture, water policy, housing, public works, energy, transport, the environment, town and country planning, nature conservation, credit, and foreign trade. It supervises the provinces, municipalities, and intercommunal utility companies.[[21]](#cite_note-fedgov3-25)

The number of Dutch-speaking [Flemish people](/wiki/Flemish_people "Flemish people") in the Capital Region is estimated to be between 11% and 15% (official figures do not exist as there is no language census and no official subnationality). According to a survey conducted by the [University of Louvain](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_catholique_de_Louvain "Université catholique de Louvain") (UCLouvain) in [Louvain-la-Neuve](/wiki/Louvain-la-Neuve "Louvain-la-Neuve") and published in June 2006, 51% of respondents from Brussels claimed to be bilingual, even if they do not have Dutch as their first language.[[22]](#cite_note-26)[[23]](#cite_note-27) They are governed by the Brussels Region for economics affairs and by the Flemish Community for educational and cultural issues.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/8/88/Brussels_-_Vlaams_Parlement.jpg/250px-Brussels_-_Vlaams_Parlement.jpg)](/wiki/File:Brussels_-_Vlaams_Parlement.jpg)

The [Flemish Parliament](/wiki/Flemish_Parliament "Flemish Parliament")

As mentioned above, Flemish institutions such as the [Flemish Parliament](/wiki/Flemish_Parliament "Flemish Parliament") and [Government](/wiki/Flemish_Government "Flemish Government"), represent the Flemish Community and the Flemish Region. The region and the community thus *de facto* share the same parliament and the same government. All these institutions are based in Brussels. Nevertheless, both types of subdivisions (the Community and the Region) still exist legally and the distinction between both is important for the people living in Brussels. Members of the Flemish Parliament who were elected in the Brussels Region cannot vote on affairs belonging to the competences of the Flemish Region.

The [official language](/wiki/Official_language "Official language") for all Flemish institutions is [Dutch](/wiki/Dutch_language "Dutch language"). French enjoys a limited official recognition in [a dozen municipalities](/wiki/Municipalities_with_language_facilities "Municipalities with language facilities") along the borders with French-speaking Wallonia, and a large recognition in the bilingual Brussels Region. French is widely known in Flanders, with 59% claiming to know French according to a survey conducted by [UCLouvain](/wiki/Universit%C3%A9_catholique_de_Louvain "Université catholique de Louvain") in [Louvain-la-Neuve](/wiki/Louvain-la-Neuve "Louvain-la-Neuve") and published in June 2006.[[24]](#cite_note-28)[[25]](#cite_note-29)

### Politics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=20 "Edit section: Politics")]

Main articles: [Politics of Flanders](/wiki/Politics_of_Flanders "Politics of Flanders") and [Political parties in Flanders](/wiki/Political_parties_in_Flanders "Political parties in Flanders")

Historically, the political parties reflected the [pillarisation](/wiki/Pillarisation "Pillarisation") (*verzuiling*) in Flemish society. The traditional political parties of the three pillars are [Christian-Democratic and Flemish](/wiki/Christian-Democratic_and_Flemish "Christian-Democratic and Flemish") (CD&V), the [Open Flemish Liberals and Democrats](/wiki/Open_Flemish_Liberals_and_Democrats "Open Flemish Liberals and Democrats") (Open Vld) and the [Socialist Party – Differently](/wiki/Socialist_Party_%E2%80%93_Differently "Socialist Party – Differently") (sp.a).

However, during the last half century, many new political parties were founded in Flanders. One of the first was the nationalist [People's Union](/wiki/People%27s_Union_(Belgium) "People's Union (Belgium)"), of which the right nationalist [Flemish Block](/wiki/Vlaams_Blok "Vlaams Blok") (now [Flemish Interest](/wiki/Vlaams_Belang "Vlaams Belang")) split off, and which later dissolved into the now-defunct Spirit or [Social Liberal Party](/wiki/Social_Liberal_Party_(Belgium) "Social Liberal Party (Belgium)"), moderate nationalism rather left of the spectrum, on the one hand, and the [New Flemish Alliance](/wiki/New_Flemish_Alliance "New Flemish Alliance") (N-VA), more conservative but independentist, on the other hand. Other parties are the leftist alternative/ecological [Green](/wiki/Groen! "Groen!") party; the short-lived anarchistic libertarian spark [ROSSEM](/wiki/ROSSEM "ROSSEM") and more recently the conservative-right liberal [List Dedecker](/wiki/Lijst_Dedecker "Lijst Dedecker"), founded by [Jean-Marie Dedecker](/wiki/Jean-Marie_Dedecker "Jean-Marie Dedecker"), and the socialist [Workers' Party](/wiki/Workers%27_Party_of_Belgium "Workers' Party of Belgium").

Particularly the [Flemish Block](/wiki/Flemish_Block "Flemish Block")/[Flemish Interest](/wiki/Flemish_Interest "Flemish Interest") has seen electoral success roughly around the turn of the century, and the [New Flemish Alliance](/wiki/New_Flemish_Alliance "New Flemish Alliance") during the last few elections, even becoming the largest party in the [2010 federal elections](/wiki/Belgian_federal_election,_2010 "Belgian federal election, 2010").

### Flemish independence

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=21 "Edit section: Flemish independence")]

Main article: [Flemish Movement](/wiki/Flemish_Movement "Flemish Movement")

2011 survey by *[Het Nieuwsblad](/wiki/Het_Nieuwsblad "Het Nieuwsblad")* and [*Koppen*](/w/index.php?title=Koppen_(TV_program)&action=edit&redlink=1 "Koppen (TV program) (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Koppen_(televisieprogramma) "nl:Koppen (televisieprogramma)")] on whether Flemings feel proud of their Belgian nationality.[[26]](#cite_note-survey-30)

1. Very proud (20.0%)
2. Proud (53.0%)
3. Not proud (18.0%)
4. Very not proud (8.00%)
5. No opinion (1.00%)

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/1/13/Menen_-_Border_crossing_1_cropped.jpg/250px-Menen_-_Border_crossing_1_cropped.jpg)](/wiki/File:Menen_-_Border_crossing_1_cropped.jpg)

Border crossing sign near [Menen](/wiki/Menen "Menen").

For some inhabitants, Flanders is more than just a geographical area or the federal institutions (Flemish Community and Region). Supporters of the Flemish Movement even call it a nation and pursue Flemish independence, but most people (approximately 75%) living in Flanders say they are proud to be Belgian and opposed to the dissolution of Belgium. 20% is even *very proud*, while some 25% are not proud and 8% is *very not proud*. Mostly students claim to be proud of their nationality, with 90% of them saying so. Of the people older than 55, 31% claim to be proud of being a Belgian. Particular opposition to secession comes from women, people employed in [services](/wiki/Service_(economics) "Service (economics)"), the highest social classes and people from big families. Strongest of all opposing the notion are housekeepers—both housewives and house husbands.[[26]](#cite_note-survey-30)

In 2012, the Flemish government drafted a "Charter for Flanders" (*Handvest voor Vlaanderen*)[[27]](#cite_note-31) of which the first article says *"Vlaanderen is een deelstaat van de federale Staat België en maakt deel uit van de Europese Unie."* ("Flanders is a [component state](/wiki/Federated_state "Federated state") of the federal State of Belgium and is part of the European Union"). In 2025, Prime Minister De Wever expressed his desire to reunite with The Netherlands, calling the Belgian separation "a disaster".[[28]](#cite_note-32)

## Geography

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=22 "Edit section: Geography")]

Further information: [Geography of Belgium](/wiki/Geography_of_Belgium "Geography of Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/39/Zoni%C3%ABnwoud_bospad_%28For%C3%AAt_de_Soignes_chemin%29_2011.jpg/250px-Zoni%C3%ABnwoud_bospad_%28For%C3%AAt_de_Soignes_chemin%29_2011.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zoni%C3%ABnwoud_bospad_(For%C3%AAt_de_Soignes_chemin)_2011.jpg)

The [Sonian Forest](/wiki/Sonian_Forest "Sonian Forest")

Flanders shares its borders with Wallonia in the south, Brussels being an enclave within the Flemish Region. The rest of the border is shared with the [Netherlands](/wiki/Netherlands "Netherlands") ([Zeelandic Flanders](/wiki/Zeelandic_Flanders "Zeelandic Flanders") in [Zeeland](/wiki/Zeeland "Zeeland"), [North Brabant](/wiki/North_Brabant "North Brabant") and [Limburg](/wiki/Limburg_(Netherlands) "Limburg (Netherlands)")) in the north and east, and with France ([French Flanders](/wiki/French_Flanders "French Flanders") in [Hauts-de-France](/wiki/Hauts-de-France "Hauts-de-France")) and the [North Sea](/wiki/North_Sea "North Sea") in the west. [Voeren](/wiki/Voeren "Voeren") is an [exclave](/wiki/Exclave "Exclave") of Flanders between Wallonia and the Netherlands, while [Baarle-Hertog](/wiki/Baarle-Hertog "Baarle-Hertog") in Flanders forms a complicated series of enclaves and exclaves with [Baarle-Nassau](/wiki/Baarle-Nassau "Baarle-Nassau") in the Netherlands. Germany, although bordering Wallonia and close to [Voeren](/wiki/Voeren "Voeren") in Limburg, does not share a border with Flanders. The [German-speaking Community of Belgium](/wiki/German-speaking_Community_of_Belgium "German-speaking Community of Belgium"), also close to Voeren, does not border Flanders either. (The commune of [Plombières](/wiki/Plombi%C3%A8res "Plombières"), majority French speaking, lies between them.)

Flanders is a highly urbanised area, lying completely within the [Blue Banana](/wiki/Blue_Banana "Blue Banana"). [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent"), [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges") and [Leuven](/wiki/Leuven "Leuven") are the largest cities of the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region"). [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") has a population of more than 500,000 citizens and is the largest city, [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent") has a population of 250,000 citizens, followed by [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges") with 120,000 citizens and [Leuven](/wiki/Leuven "Leuven") counts almost 100,000 citizens.[[29]](#cite_note-33)

[Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels") is a part of Flanders as far as community matters are concerned, but does not belong to the Flemish Region.

Flanders has two main geographical regions: the coastal [Yser](/wiki/Yser "Yser") basin plain in the north-west and a central plain. The first consists mainly of sand dunes and [clayey](/wiki/Clay "Clay") [alluvial](/wiki/Alluvial "Alluvial") soils in the [polders](/wiki/Polder "Polder"). Polders are areas of land, close to or below sea level that have been reclaimed from the sea, from which they are protected by [dikes](/wiki/Dike_(construction) "Dike (construction)") or, a little further inland, by fields that have been drained with canals. With similar soils along the lowermost [Scheldt](/wiki/Scheldt "Scheldt") basin starts the central plain, a smooth, slowly rising fertile area irrigated by many waterways that reaches an average height of about five metres (16 feet) above sea level with wide valleys of its rivers upstream as well as the [Campine](/wiki/Campine "Campine") region to the east having sandy soils at altitudes around thirty metres.[[e]](#cite_note-34) Near its southern edges close to Wallonia one can find slightly rougher land, richer in [calcium](/wiki/Calcium "Calcium"), with low hills reaching up to 150 m (490 ft) and small valleys, and at the eastern border with the Netherlands, in the [Meuse](/wiki/Meuse "Meuse") basin, there are [marl](/wiki/Marl "Marl") caves (*mergelgrotten*). Its [exclave](/wiki/Exclave "Exclave") around [Voeren](/wiki/Voeren "Voeren") between the Dutch border and Wallonia's [Liège Province](/wiki/Li%C3%A8ge_Province "Liège Province") attains a maximum altitude of 288 m (945 ft) above sea level.[[30]](#cite_note-35)[[31]](#cite_note-inbo-36)

The province of Flemish Brabant is the most recently created, being formed in 1995 after the splitting of the [province of Brabant](/wiki/Province_of_Brabant "Province of Brabant") on a linguistic basis.

Most municipalities are made up of several former municipalities, now called *[deelgemeenten](/wiki/Deelgemeente "Deelgemeente")*. The largest municipality (both in terms of population and area) is [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp"), having more than half a million inhabitants. Its nine *deelgemeenten* have a special status and are called [districts](/wiki/Districts_of_Antwerp "Districts of Antwerp"), which have an elected council and a college. While any municipality with more than 100,000 inhabitants can establish districts, only Antwerp did this so far. The smallest municipality (also both in terms of population and area) is [Herstappe](/wiki/Herstappe "Herstappe") (Limburg).[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/c/cd/Bruxelles_Brussels-Capital_Belgium_Map.svg/250px-Bruxelles_Brussels-Capital_Belgium_Map.svg.png)](/wiki/File:Bruxelles_Brussels-Capital_Belgium_Map.svg)

Brussels-Capital Region with the [City of Brussels](/wiki/City_of_Brussels "City of Brussels") (one of 19 municipalities) in red

The **[Flemish Community](/wiki/Flemish_Community "Flemish Community")** covers both the [Flemish Region](/wiki/Flemish_Region "Flemish Region") and, together with the French Community, the [Brussels-Capital Region](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels"). Brussels, an enclave within the province of Flemish Brabant, is not divided into any province nor is it part of any. It coincides with the [Arrondissement of Brussels-Capital](/wiki/Arrondissement_of_Brussels-Capital "Arrondissement of Brussels-Capital") and includes [19 municipalities](/wiki/List_of_municipalities_of_the_Brussels-Capital_Region "List of municipalities of the Brussels-Capital Region").

The [Flemish Government](/wiki/Flemish_Government "Flemish Government") has its own local institutions in the Brussels-Capital Region, being the *[Vlaamse Gemeenschapscommissie](/wiki/Flemish_Community_Commission "Flemish Community Commission")* (VGC), and its municipal antennae (*Gemeenschapscentra*, community centres for the Flemish community in Brussels). These institutions are independent from the educational, cultural and social institutions that depend directly on the Flemish Government. They exert, among others, all those cultural competences that outside Brussels fall under the provinces.

## Climate

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=24 "Edit section: Climate")]

The climate is maritime [temperate](/wiki/Temperate "Temperate"), with significant precipitation in all seasons ([Köppen climate classification](/wiki/K%C3%B6ppen_climate_classification "Köppen climate classification"): *Cfb*; the average temperature is 3 °C (37 °F) in January, and 21 °C (70 °F) in July; the average precipitation is 65 millimetres (2.6 inches) in January, and 78 millimetres (3.1 inches) in July).

## Economy

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=25 "Edit section: Economy")]

Main articles: [Economy of Belgium](/wiki/Economy_of_Belgium "Economy of Belgium"), [Flemish Diamond](/wiki/Flemish_Diamond "Flemish Diamond"), [Science and technology in Flanders](/wiki/Science_and_technology_in_Flanders "Science and technology in Flanders"), and [Agriculture in Flanders](/wiki/Agriculture_in_Flanders "Agriculture in Flanders")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/b/be/Zicht_op_het_Delwaidedok.jpg/250px-Zicht_op_het_Delwaidedok.jpg)](/wiki/File:Zicht_op_het_Delwaidedok.jpg)

The Port of Antwerp is the second-largest in Europe.

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/7/77/Lijn11_a12.jpg/250px-Lijn11_a12.jpg)](/wiki/File:Lijn11_a12.jpg)

The [A12](/wiki/A12_road_(Belgium) "A12 road (Belgium)") with a railway in the centre.

Total [gross regional product](/wiki/Gross_regional_product "Gross regional product") (GRP) of Flanders in 2021 was €296 billion (excluding Brussels).[[33]](#cite_note-38) Per capita GDP at [purchasing power parity](/wiki/Purchasing_power_parity "Purchasing power parity") was 20% above the EU average.[[34]](#cite_note-39) Flemish productivity per capita is about 13% higher than that in Wallonia, and wages are about 7% higher than in Wallonia.[[35]](#cite_note-40)

Flanders was one of the first continental European areas to undergo the [Industrial Revolution](/wiki/Industrial_Revolution "Industrial Revolution"), in the 19th century. Initially, the modernization relied heavily on food processing and textile. However, by the 1840s the textile industry of Flanders was in severe crisis and there was famine in Flanders (1846–50). After World War II, [Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp") and [Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent") experienced a rapid expansion of the chemical and petroleum industries. Flanders also attracted a large majority of foreign investments in Belgium. The [1973](/wiki/1973_oil_crisis "1973 oil crisis") and [1979 oil crises](/wiki/1979_oil_crisis "1979 oil crisis") sent the economy into a recession. The steel industry remained in relatively good shape. In the 1980s and 90s, the economic centre of Belgium continued to shift further to Flanders and is now concentrated in the populous [Flemish Diamond](/wiki/Flemish_Diamond "Flemish Diamond") area.[[36]](#cite_note-41) Nowadays, the Flemish economy is mainly service-oriented.

Belgium is a founding member of the [European Coal and Steel Community](/wiki/European_Coal_and_Steel_Community "European Coal and Steel Community") in 1951, which evolved into the present-day [European Union](/wiki/European_Union "European Union"). In 1999, the [euro](/wiki/Euro "Euro"), the single European currency, was introduced in Flanders. It replaced the [Belgian franc](/wiki/Belgian_franc "Belgian franc") in 2002.

The Flemish economy is strongly export-oriented, in particular of high value-added goods.[[37]](#cite_note-42) The main imports are food products, machinery, rough diamonds, petroleum and petroleum products, chemicals, clothing and accessories, and textiles. The main exports are automobiles, food and food products, iron and steel, finished diamonds, textiles, plastics, petroleum products, and non-ferrous metals. Since 1922, Belgium and Luxembourg have been a single trade market within a [customs](/wiki/Customs_union "Customs union") and [currency union](/wiki/Currency_union "Currency union")—the [Belgium–Luxembourg Economic Union](/wiki/Belgium%E2%80%93Luxembourg_Economic_Union "Belgium–Luxembourg Economic Union"). Its main trading partners are Germany, the Netherlands, France, the United Kingdom, Italy, the United States, and Spain.[[38]](#cite_note-43)

Antwerp is the number one diamond market in the world, diamond exports account for roughly 1/10 of Belgian exports. The Antwerp-based [BASF](/wiki/BASF "BASF") plant is the largest BASF base outside Germany, and accounts on its own for about 2% of Belgian exports. Other industrial and service activities in Antwerp include car manufacturing, telecommunications, and photographic products.

Flanders is home to several science and technology institutes, such as [IMEC](/wiki/IMEC "IMEC"), VITO, Flanders DC, and [Flanders Make](/w/index.php?title=Flanders_Make&action=edit&redlink=1 "Flanders Make (page does not exist)").

### Infrastructure

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=26 "Edit section: Infrastructure")]

Main article: [Transport in Belgium](/wiki/Transport_in_Belgium "Transport in Belgium")

Flanders has developed an extensive transportation infrastructure of ports, canals, railways and highways. The [Port of Antwerp](/wiki/Port_of_Antwerp "Port of Antwerp") is the second-largest in Europe, after [Rotterdam](/wiki/Port_of_Rotterdam "Port of Rotterdam").[[39]](#cite_note-44) Other ports are [Bruges-Zeebrugge](/wiki/Port_of_Bruges-Zeebrugge "Port of Bruges-Zeebrugge"), [Ghent](/wiki/Port_of_Ghent "Port of Ghent") and [Ostend](/wiki/Port_of_Ostend "Port of Ostend"), of which Zeebrugge and Ostend are located at the [Belgian coast](/w/index.php?title=Belgian_coast&action=edit&redlink=1 "Belgian coast (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Belgische_Kust "nl:Belgische Kust")].

Whereas railways are managed by the federal [National Railway Company of Belgium](/wiki/National_Railway_Company_of_Belgium "National Railway Company of Belgium"), other public transport ([De Lijn](/wiki/De_Lijn "De Lijn")) and roads are managed by the Flemish region.

The main airport is [Brussels Airport](/wiki/Brussels_Airport "Brussels Airport"), the only other civilian airport with scheduled services in Flanders is [Antwerp International Airport](/wiki/Antwerp_International_Airport "Antwerp International Airport"), but there are two other ones with cargo or charter flights: [Ostend-Bruges International Airport](/wiki/Ostend-Bruges_International_Airport "Ostend-Bruges International Airport") and [Kortrijk-Wevelgem International Airport](/wiki/Kortrijk-Wevelgem_International_Airport "Kortrijk-Wevelgem International Airport"), both in West Flanders.

## Demographics

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=27 "Edit section: Demographics")]

Further information: [Demographics of Belgium](/wiki/Demographics_of_Belgium "Demographics of Belgium"), [Flemish people](/wiki/Flemish_people "Flemish people"), and [List of cities in Flanders](/wiki/List_of_cities_in_Flanders "List of cities in Flanders")

The highest population density is found in the area circumscribed by the [Brussels](/wiki/Brussels "Brussels")-[Antwerp](/wiki/Antwerp "Antwerp")-[Ghent](/wiki/Ghent "Ghent")-[Leuven](/wiki/Leuven "Leuven") agglomerations that surround Mechelen and is known as the [Flemish Diamond](/wiki/Flemish_Diamond "Flemish Diamond"), in other important urban centres as [Bruges](/wiki/Bruges "Bruges"), [Roeselare](/wiki/Roeselare "Roeselare") and [Kortrijk](/wiki/Kortrijk "Kortrijk") to the west, and notable centres [Turnhout](/wiki/Turnhout "Turnhout") and [Hasselt](/wiki/Hasselt "Hasselt") to the east. On 1 January 2015, the Flemish Region had a population of 6,444,127 and about 20% of the 1,175,173 people in the Brussels Region are also considered Flemish.[[f]](#cite_note-Note_Nationality-Minority-Education-45)[[40]](#cite_note-statbel-46)[*[citation needed](/wiki/Wikipedia:Citation_needed "Wikipedia:Citation needed")*]

### Religion

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=28 "Edit section: Religion")]

Further information: [Religion in Belgium](/wiki/Religion_in_Belgium "Religion in Belgium")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/3/3f/Houthalen_-_Onze-Lieve-Vrouw_van_Zeven_Wee%C3%ABnkerk.jpg/250px-Houthalen_-_Onze-Lieve-Vrouw_van_Zeven_Wee%C3%ABnkerk.jpg)](/wiki/File:Houthalen_-_Onze-Lieve-Vrouw_van_Zeven_Wee%C3%ABnkerk.jpg)

A church in [Houthalen](/wiki/Houthalen "Houthalen"). A typical church, similar to those in many villages in Flanders

The Belgian constitution provides for [freedom of religion](/wiki/Freedom_of_religion_in_Belgium "Freedom of religion in Belgium"), and the various governments in general respect this right in practice. Since independence, [Catholicism](/wiki/Catholicism "Catholicism"), counterbalanced by strong [freethought](/wiki/Freethought "Freethought") movements, has had an important role in Belgium's politics, since the 20th century in Flanders mainly via the Christian trade union [ACV](/wiki/Confederation_of_Christian_Trade_Unions "Confederation of Christian Trade Unions") and the [Christian Democratic and Flemish](/wiki/Christen-Democratisch_en_Vlaams "Christen-Democratisch en Vlaams") party (CD&V). According to the *2001 Survey and Study of Religion*, about 47 percent of the Belgian population identify themselves as belonging to the Catholic Church, while Islam is the second-largest religion at 3.5 percent.[[41]](#cite_note-47) A 2006 inquiry in Flanders, considered more religious than Wallonia, showed that 55% considered themselves religious, and 36% believed that God created the world.[[42]](#cite_note-48)

Jews [have been present](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Belgium "History of the Jews in Belgium") in Flanders for a long time, in particular [in Antwerp](/wiki/History_of_the_Jews_in_Antwerp "History of the Jews in Antwerp"). More recently, Muslims have immigrated to Flanders, now forming the largest minority religion with about 3.9% in the Flemish Region and 25% in Brussels.[[43]](#cite_note-49) The largest Muslim group is Moroccan in origin, while the second-largest is Turkish in origin.

### Education

[[edit](/w/index.php?title=Flanders&action=edit&section=29 "Edit section: Education")]

Further information: [Education in Flanders](/wiki/Education_in_Flanders "Education in Flanders")

[![](//upload.wikimedia.org/wikipedia/commons/thumb/0/0e/Castle_Arenberg%2C_Katholieke_Universiteit_Leuven_adj.jpg/250px-Castle_Arenberg%2C_Katholieke_Universiteit_Leuven_adj.jpg)](/wiki/File:Castle_Arenberg,_Katholieke_Universiteit_Leuven_adj.jpg)

[Arenberg Castle](/wiki/Arenberg_Castle "Arenberg Castle"), part of the [Katholieke Universiteit Leuven](/wiki/Katholieke_Universiteit_Leuven "Katholieke Universiteit Leuven"), the oldest university in Belgium and the Low Countries.

Education is compulsory from the ages of six to 18, but most [Flemings](/wiki/Flemish_people "Flemish people") continue to study until around 23. Among the [Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development](/wiki/Organisation_for_Economic_Co-operation_and_Development "Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development") countries in 1999, Flanders had the third-highest proportion of 18- to 21-year-olds enrolled in [postsecondary education](/wiki/Postsecondary_education "Postsecondary education"). Flanders also scores very high in international comparative studies on education. Its secondary school students consistently rank among the top three for mathematics and science. However, the success is not evenly spread: ethnic minority youth score consistently lower, and the difference is larger than in most comparable countries.[[f]](#cite_note-Note_Nationality-Minority-Education-45)

Mirroring the historical political conflicts between the secular and Catholic segments of the population, the Flemish educational system is split into a secular branch controlled by the communities, the provinces, or the municipalities, and a [subsidised](/wiki/Subsidy "Subsidy") religious—mostly Catholic—branch. For the subsidised schools, the main costs such as the teacher's wages and building maintenance completely borne by the Flemish government. Subsidised schools are also free to determine their own teaching and examination methods, but in exchange, they must be able to prove that certain minimal terms are achieved by keeping records of the given lessons and exams. It should however be noted that—at least for the Catholic schools—the religious authorities have very limited power over these schools, neither do the schools have a lot of power on their own. Instead, the Catholic schools are a member of the Catholic umbrella organisation [VSKO](/w/index.php?title=VSKO&action=edit&redlink=1 "VSKO (page does not exist)") [[nl](https://nl.wikipedia.org/wiki/Katholiek_Onderwijs_Vlaanderen "nl:Katholiek Onderwijs Vlaanderen")]. The VSKO determines most practicalities for schools, like the advised schedules per study field. However, there's freedom of education in Flanders, which doesn't only mean that every pupil can choose his/her preferred school, but also that every organisation can found a school, and even be subsidised when abiding the different rules. This resulted also in some smaller school systems follow 'methodical pedagogies' (e.g. [Steiner](/wiki/Waldorf_education "Waldorf education"), [Montessori](/wiki/Montessori "Montessori"), or [Freinet](/wiki/Freinet "Freinet")) or serve the Jewish and Protestant minorities.

During the school year 2003–2004, 68.30% of the total population of children between the ages of six and 18 went to subsidized private schools (both religious schools or 'methodical pedagogies' schools).[[44]](#cite_note-50)

The big freedom given to schools results in a constant competition to be the "best" school. The schools get certain reputations amongst parents and employers. So it's important for schools to be the best school since the subsidies depend on the number of pupils. This competition has been pinpointed as one of the main reasons for the high overall quality of the Flemish education. However, the importance of a school's reputation also makes schools more eager to expel pupils that don't perform well. Resulting in the ethnic differences and the well-known waterfall system: pupils start high in the perceived hierarchy, and then drop towards more professional oriented directions or "easier" schools when they can't handle the pressure any longer.
